title: Sets
description: Go has no built-in set type. Use map[T]bool or map[T]struct{} to simulate sets — essential for deduplication, membership checks, and visited tracking in graph problems.

sections.title: Set with map[T]bool
sections.explanation: The simplest set pattern: **map[T]bool** where the value is `true` for present elements. Check membership with `if set[key]` — missing keys return `false` (the zero value), so no comma-ok needed. This is the most readable approach for interviews.

exercise.prompt: Create a set of ints containing 1, 2, 3 using map[int]bool. Add 4 to the set. Check if 2 is in the set and if 5 is in the set (print both booleans). Print the size.

validation.message: 2 is in the set (true), 5 is not (false), size is 4 after adding
Use map[int]bool for the set and assign true to add elements

exercise.hint: set[4] = true adds 4. set[2] returns true (present), set[5] returns false (missing, zero value).

sections.title: Memory-efficient set with struct{}
sections.explanation: For large sets, **map[T]struct{}** uses zero bytes per value instead of 1 byte for bool. Check membership with the **comma-ok** idiom: `_, ok := set[key]`. This is the idiomatic Go pattern for performance-sensitive code, but map[T]bool is simpler for interviews.

exercise.prompt: Create a set using map[string]struct{} and add "go", "python", "java". Use comma-ok to check if "go" exists (print "found") and if "rust" is missing (print "not found"). Print the size.

validation.message: "go" is in the set, "rust" is not, size is 3
Use struct{} as value type and comma-ok for membership checks

exercise.hint: map[string]struct{}{} creates an empty set. Add with set[key] = struct{}{}. Check with _, ok := set[key]; ok.

sections.title: Set operations: union, intersection, difference
sections.explanation: Go doesn't have built-in set operations, so you implement them with loops. **Union**: iterate both sets, add all to result. **Intersection**: iterate one, check in the other. **Difference**: iterate first, skip if in second. These are common in interview follow-up questions.

exercise.prompt: Given sets a = {1,2,3,4} and b = {3,4,5,6}, find and print their intersection (elements in both). Collect the results in a sorted slice and print it.

validation.message: Iterate set a, check membership in b, sort for deterministic output

exercise.hint: Loop over a with range, check if b[k] is true. Append matches to result. Sort for consistent output.

sections.title: Visited tracking pattern
sections.explanation: In graph/tree problems, a **visited set** prevents revisiting nodes. Use `map[int]bool` or `map[[2]int]bool` for grid coordinates. Check before processing, add immediately after. This pattern is used in BFS, DFS, and flood-fill problems.

exercise.prompt: Given edges as a map, do a BFS from node 0 and print each visited node. Use a map[int]bool as visited set. Nodes: 0->[1,2], 1->[3], 2->[3], 3->[].

validation.message: BFS from 0 visits 0, 1, 2, 3 in level order
Use map[int]bool for visited tracking with check-and-add pattern

exercise.hint: Check !visited[nb] before adding to queue. Set visited[nb] = true immediately after.
